phrasalVerbs.definition: To create a situation in which people meet and do something together.
examples: The conference brought together experts from around the world.
Music has the power to bring together people from all backgrounds.
The project brought together volunteers from different organisations.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To change your opinion or decision because someone has persuaded you.
examples: He eventually came around to my way of thinking.
Give her time—she'll come round to the idea.
The board finally came around to accepting the proposal.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To cause a disagreement or argument between people.
examples: Don't let money come between you and your friends.
Nothing should come between family members.
The inheritance dispute came between the siblings.

phrasalVerbs.definition: If something or someone grows on you, you start to like them more.
examples: The neighbourhood has really grown on me over the years.
Classical music didn't appeal to me at first, but it's grown on me.
He was annoying initially, but he's grown on me.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To become friends with someone again after a disagreement; to give basic medical treatment.
examples: They managed to patch up their differences after the argument.
The nurse patched up his wound before the doctor arrived.
It took years, but they finally patched up their friendship.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To begin to like someone or something; to start doing something as a habit.
examples: The children took to their new teacher immediately.
He's taken to jogging every morning before work.
She really took to the new hobby and spends hours on it.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To leave a meeting before the end, usually because you are angry; to suddenly leave a relationship.
examples: Several delegates walked out in protest during the speech.
She walked out on her husband after discovering his lies.
The union members threatened to walk out of negotiations.

fillInExercises.sentence: The festival ___ musicians from over fifty countries.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Bring together' means to create an occasion where people unite.

fillInExercises.sentence: She eventually ___ round to accepting the job offer.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Come round to' means to change your mind and agree.

fillInExercises.sentence: The disagreement about money ___ the two old friends.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Come between' means to cause conflict or separation.

fillInExercises.sentence: The city has really ___ on me since I moved here.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Grow on' means to gradually become more likeable to someone.

fillInExercises.sentence: After the falling out, it took months to ___ their relationship.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Patch up' means to repair or mend a relationship.

fillInExercises.sentence: The new puppy ___ to its owner right away.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Take to' means to begin to like someone immediately.

fillInExercises.sentence: Several board members ___ of the meeting in protest.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Walk out' means to leave suddenly, often in anger.

fillInExercises.sentence: Don't let jealousy ___ between you and your sister.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Come between' means to cause division or conflict.

lines.speaker: Chen
lines.text: Have you and Sarah [patched up|patch up] your disagreement yet?

lines.speaker: Maria
lines.text: Not completely. It's hard when misunderstandings [come between|come between] friends.

lines.speaker: Chen
lines.text: The reunion event might [bring together|bring together] everyone again.

lines.speaker: Maria
lines.text: Maybe. She's slowly [came (a)round (to)|come (a)round (to)] talking to me.

lines.speaker: Chen
lines.text: Give it time. People often [grow on|grow on] us again after conflicts.

lines.speaker: Maria
lines.text: I just hope she doesn't [walk out|walk out] when she sees me there.

lines.speaker: Oliver
lines.text: Have the new neighbours [grew on|grow on] you yet?

lines.speaker: Clara
lines.text: Actually, yes! The kids really [took to|take to] them immediately.

lines.speaker: Oliver
lines.text: That's great. Shared interests can [bring together|bring together] families.

lines.speaker: Clara
lines.text: I was sceptical at first, but I've [come (a)round (to)|come (a)round (to)] them.

lines.speaker: Oliver
lines.text: Let's hope nothing [comes between|come between] you in the future.

lines.speaker: Clara
lines.text: We'll [patch up|patch up] any differences quickly if they arise.
